author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
title: The Sixteenth Breviate.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: After all these disputations, their author introduces himself in person as arguing with another, and represents himself as under examination, and as being addressed by his examiner: “Show me the man who is without sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: He answers: “I show you one who is able to be without sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: His examiner then says to him: “And who is he?”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: “But if,” he adds, “you were to say, ‘I, at any rate, cannot be without sin,’ then you must answer me, ‘Whose fault is that?’

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: If you then were to say, ‘My own fault,’ you must be further asked, ‘And how is it your fault, if you cannot be without sin?’”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: He again represents himself as under examination, and thus accosted: “Are you yourself without sin, who say that a man can be without sin?”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: And he answers: “Whose fault is it that I am not without sin?

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: But if,” continues he, “he had said in reply, ‘The fault is your own;’ then the answer would be, ‘How my fault, when I am unable to be without sin?’”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Now our answer to all this running argument is, that no controversy ought to have been raised between them about such words as these; because he nowhere ventures to affirm that a man (either any one else, or himself) is without sin, but he merely said in reply that he can be,—a position which we do not ourselves deny.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Only the question arises, when can he, and through whom can he?

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: If at the present time, then by no faithful soul which is enclosed within the body of this death must this prayer be offered, or such words as these be spoken, “Forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors,” since in holy baptism all past debts have been already forgiven.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: But whoever tries to persuade us that such a prayer is not proper for faithful members of Christ, does in fact acknowledge nothing else than that he is not himself a Christian.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: If, again, it is through himself that a man is able to live without sin, then did Christ die in vain.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: But “Christ is not dead in vain.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: No man, therefore, can be without sin, even if he wish it, unless he be assisted by the grace of God through our Lord Jesus Christ.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: And that this perfection may be attained, there is even now a training carried on in growing [Christians,] and there will be by all means a completion made, after the conflict with death is spent, and love, which is now cherished by the operation of faith and hope, shall be perfected in the fruition of sight and possession.
